volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: Pseudolus (Section 1.1.81 - Section 1.2.196)

sections.label: Section 1.1.81
sections.original: Eheu .
sections.translation: Alas !

sections.label: Section 1.1.81b
sections.original: Neque intus nummus ullus est .
sections.translation: Nor is there a single coin in the house .

sections.label: Section 1.1.81c
sections.original: Eheu .
sections.translation: Alas !

sections.label: Section 1.1.82
sections.original: Ille abducturus est mulierem cras .
sections.translation: He ’ s going to carry the damsel away to-morrow .

sections.label: Section 1.1.82b
sections.original: Eheu .
sections.translation: Alas !

sections.label: Section 1.1.83
sections.original: Istocine pacto me adiuvas ?
sections.translation: Is it in that fashion that you help me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.83b
sections.original: Do id quod mihi est ;
sections.translation: I give you that which I have ; for I ’ ve a perpetual supply of those treasures [*] in my house .

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: Actum est de me hodie . sed potes nunc mutuam
sections.translation: It ’ s all over with me this very day . But can you now lend me one drachma , which I ’ ll pay you back to-morrow

sections.label: Section 1.1.87
sections.original: Vix hercle , opinor , si me opponam pignori .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I hardly think I could , even though I should pawn myself for it . But what do you want to do with this drachma ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.88b
sections.original: Restim volo
sections.translation: I want to purchase a halter for myself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.89b
sections.original: Quam ob rem ?
sections.translation: For what reason ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.89c
sections.original: Qui me faciam pensilem .
sections.translation: With which to hang myself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: certum est mihi ante tenebras tenebras persequi .
sections.translation: I ’ m determined , ere ’ tis dark , to take [*] a leap in the dark .

sections.label: Section 1.1.91
sections.original: Quis mi igitur drachumam reddet , si dedero tibi ?
sections.translation: Who then shall pay me back my drachma * * * * ? Do you wish purposely to hang yourself for the very reason , that you may cheat mo out of my drachma if I lend it you ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.94
sections.original: Profecto nullo pacto possum vivere ,
sections.translation: At all events , I can in nowise survive

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: si illa a me abalienatur atque abducitur .
sections.translation: if she ’ s removed and carried off from me .

sections.label: Line 96
sections.original: Quid fles , cucule ? vives .
sections.translation: Why do you weep , you cuckoo [*] ? You shall survive .

sections.label: Section 1.1.96b
sections.original: Quid ego ni fleam ,
sections.translation: Why should I not weep , who have neither a coin of silver in ready money , nor have the hope of a groat [*] anywhere in the world ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.99
sections.original: Vt litterarum ego harum sermonem audio ,
sections.translation: As I understand the tenor of this letter ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: nisi tu illi lacrumis fleveris argenteis ,
sections.translation: unless you weep for her with tears of silver , the affection which you wish yourself by those tears to prove is of no more value than if you were to pour water into a sieve . But have no fear , I ’ ll not forsake you in your love . In troth , I do trust that this day , from some quarter or other , by my good aid

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.original: tibi inventurum esse auxilium argentarium .
sections.translation: I shall find you help in the money line . But whence that is to come ,— that whence I know not how to pronounce ; except only that so it shall be ; my eyebrow twitches [*] to that effect .

sections.label: Section 1.1.108
sections.original: Vtinam quae dicis dictis facta suppetant .
sections.translation: As to what you say , I trust that your deeds may be as good as your words .

sections.label: Section 1.1.109
sections.original: Scis tu quidem hercle , mea si commovi sacra ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you surely know , if I set my plans a-going [*] ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.110
sections.original: quo pacto et quantas soleam turbellas dare .
sections.translation: after what fashion and how great is the bustle that I am in the habit of causing .

sections.label: Section 1.1.111
sections.original: In te nunc omnes spes sunt aetati meae .
sections.translation: In you are now centred all the hopes of my existence .

sections.label: Section 1.1.112
sections.original: Satin est , si hanc hodie mulierem efficio tibi
sections.translation: Is it enough , if I this day make this damsel to be yours , or if I find you twenty minae ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.114
sections.original: Satis , sí futurumst .
sections.translation: ’ Tis enough , if so it is to be .

sections.label: Section 1.1.114b
sections.original: Roga me viginti minas ,
sections.translation: Ask of me twenty minae ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.115
sections.original: ut me effecturum tibi quod promisi scias .
sections.translation: that you may be assured that I ’ ll procure for you that which I have promised . Ask them of me , by my troth , prithee do ; I long to make the promise .

sections.label: Section 1.1.117
sections.original: Dabisne argenti mi hodie viginti minas ?
sections.translation: Will you this day find me twenty minae of silver ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.118
sections.original: Dabo . molestus nunciam ne sis mihi .
sections.translation: I will find them ; be no more troublesome to me then . And this I tell you first , that you mayn ’ t deny that it was told you ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.120
sections.original: si neminem alium potero , tuom tangam patrem .
sections.translation: if I can no one else , I ’ ll diddle your father out of the money .

sections.label: Section 1.1.121
sections.original: Di te mihi semper servent . verum , si potest ,
sections.translation: So far as filial affection is concerned , even my mother as well . May the Gods always preserve you for me . But what if you are not able ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.123
sections.original: De istac re in oculum utrumvis conquiescito .
sections.translation: Upon that matter do you go to sleep with either eye .

sections.label: Section 1.1.124
sections.original: Oculum anne in aurem ?
sections.translation: With the eye or with the ear [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.124b
sections.original: At hoc pervolgatumst minus .
sections.translation: The latter is too common an expression .

sections.label: Section 1.1.125
sections.original: nunc , ne quis dictum sibi neget , dico omnibus
sections.translation: Now , that no one may affirm that it wasn ’ t told him , I tell you all ( to the AUDIENCE ) , in the presence of the youths in this audience , and of all the people , to all my friends and all my acquaintances I give notice , that for this day they must guard against me , and not trust me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.129_130
sections.original: St , táce opsecro hércle .
sections.translation: Hist ! be silent , prithee , by all the powers !

sections.label: Section 1.1.129_130b
sections.original: Quid negoti est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.129_130c
sections.original: Ostium
sections.translation: There was a noise at the procurer ’ s door .

sections.label: Section 1.1.131b
sections.original: Crura mavellem modo .
sections.translation: I could only wish it were his legs in preference .

sections.label: Section 1.1.132
sections.original: Atque ipse egreditur intus , periuri caput .
sections.translation: Yes , and he himself is coming out from in-doors , the perjured scoundrel .

sections.label: Section 1.2.133
sections.original: Exíte , agite exite , ígnavi , male hábiti et male concíliati ,
sections.translation: Get out , come , out with you , you rascals , kept at a loss and bought at a loss , in the minds of not one of whom aught ever comes to do aright ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: quibus , nísi ad hoc exemplum éxperior , non pótest usura usúrpari .
sections.translation: of whom I can ’ t make a bit of use , unless I try it after this fashion . ( He flogs the men all round .) At no time did I ever see human beings more like asses ; so hardened are your ribs with stripes ; when you flog them , you hurt yourself the most . Of such a disposition are these whipping-posts who follow this line of conduct ; when the opportunity is given , pilfer , purloin [*] , prig , plunder , drink , eat , and run away ’ s the word . This

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: ést eorum opus , ut mávelis lúpos apud ovis línquere ,
sections.translation: is their method , so that you would choose rather to leave wolves among sheep , than these fellows on guard in your house . Yet , when you look at their appearance , they don ’ t seem amiss ; by their doings they deceive you . Now , therefore , unless you all of you give your attention to this charge , unless you remove drowsiness and sloth from your breasts and eyes ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: ita ego vestra latera loris faciam ut valide varia sint ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll make your sides to be right thoroughly marked with thongs , so much so that not even Campanian coverlets are coloured as well , nor yet Alexandrian tapestry [*] of purple embroidered with beasts all over . Even yesterday I already gave you all notice , and assigned to each his own respective employment ; but so utterly worthless are you , so neglectful , of such stubborn dispositions ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: officium vestrum ut vos malo cogatis commonerier ;
sections.translation: that you compel me to put you in mind of your duty with a basting . You are so minded I suppose , to get the better of this scourge and myself through the hardness of your hides . Never , i ’ faith , will your hides prove harder , than is this cow-hide of mine . ( He dangles it before them .) Do look at that , please ; they are minding other matters . Attend to this , and give heed to this .

sections.label: Section 1.2.155
sections.original: quid nunc ? doletne ? em sic datur , si quis erum servos spernit .
sections.translation: ( He flogs one of them .) How now ? Does it pain ? Ah , that ’ s the way it ’ s laid on when any slave slights his master . Stand all of you before me , you race of mortals born to be thrashed ; turn your ears this way ; give attention all of you to what I say . You fellow who are holding the pitcher , do you fetch the water ; do you take care that the cauldron ’ s full this instant . You , with the axe , I appoint over the wood-cutting department .

sections.label: Section 1.2.159
sections.original: At haec retunsast .
sections.translation: But this one is blunted on the edge .

sections.label: Section 1.2.159b
sections.original: Sine siet ; itidem vos quoque estis plagis omnes :
sections.translation: Let it be so , then . And so are you yourselves with stripes ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.160
sections.original: numqui minus ea gratia tamen omnium opera útor ?
sections.translation: yet am I for that reason any the less to enjoy your services ? My orders I give to you , that the house be made clean . You have what you are to do ; make haste , and go in-doors . ( Exit FIRST SLAVE .) Be you the one that makes the couches smooth [*] . Do you wash the plate clean , and arrange it in order as well , Take care that when I return from the Forum , I find things done ; that all be swept , sprinkled , scoured , made smooth , cleaned , and arranged in order .

sections.label: Section 1.2.165
sections.original: nam mi hódie natalís dies est , decet éum omnis vos concélebrare .
sections.translation: For this day is my birthday ; it befits you all to celebrate it . Take care to lay the gammon of bacon , the brawn , the collared neck , and the udder , in water ; do you hear me ? I wish to entertain tip-top men in first-rate style , that they may fancy that I have property . Go you in-doors , and get these things ready quickly , that there may be no delay when the cook comes . I ’ m going to market , that I may make purchase of whatever fish is there .

sections.label: Section 1.2.170
sections.original: i , púere , prae ; ne quísquam pertundát cruminam caútiost .
sections.translation: Boy , go you before me ; I must have a care that no one cuts away my purse . Or wait there ; there ’ s something that I had almost forgotten to say at home . Do you hear me , you women ? I have this charge for you — you , misses of distinction , who spend your time with illustrious men in refinements , luxury , and delights ; now shall I know and make trial this day ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.175
sections.original: quae cápiti , quae ventri óperam det , quae suaé rei , quae somnó studeat ;
sections.translation: which one has regard for her liberty [*] , which for her appetite which thinks on her business , which on sleeping only : this day I ’ ll make trial which I must think of as a freed-woman , and which as one to be sold . Take you care that many a present from your lovers comes in for me this day ; for if your year ’ s board isn ’ t picked up for me , to-morrow I ’ ll turn you adrift on the public . You know that this is my birthday ; where are those youths , the apples of whose eyes you are ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.180
sections.original: quibus vítae , quibus delíciae estis , quibus sávia , mammia , méllillae ?
sections.translation: whose very existence , whose delight you are ? Where are your kisses , where your bosoms sweet as honey ? Make the bearers of presents to come here then , for my sake , before this house , in whole regiments [*] . Why am I to find clothes for you , gold trinkets , and those things which you need ? What have I , you jades , through your means , except vexation , you women , eager for nothing but the wine ? You are a-soaking away yourselves and your paunches too , at the very time that I ’ m here a-dry .

sections.label: Section 1.2.185
sections.original: nunc ádeo hoc factust óptumum , ut nomíne quemque appellem suo ,
sections.translation: Now , therefore , this is the best thing to do ; for me to call you each by her name , that no one of you may be declaring to me by-and-by that her business hasn ’ t been told her . Give attention , all of you . In the first place , Hedylium , my business is with you — you , who are the favorite of the corn-merchants , men who have , all of them , immense mountains of wheat piled up at home ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.190
sections.original: fac sís sit delatum húc mihi fruméntum , hunc annum quód satis ,
sections.translation: take you care that wheat is brought here for me , to suffice this year to come for myself and all my household , and that I may so abound in corn that the city may change my name for me , and instead of the procurer Ballio proclaim me King lasions [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.194
sections.original: Aúdin , furcifér quae loquitur ? sátin magnificus tíbi videtur ?
sections.translation: Do you hear what the gallows-bird is saying ? ⋯ Doesn ’ t he seem a regular boaster to you ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.195
sections.original: Pól iste , atque etiam málificus .
sections.translation: I ’ troth the fellow does , and a wicked one [*] as well . But hush now , and give attention to this .

sections.label: Section 1.2.196
sections.original: Aéschrodora , tú quae amicos tíbi habes lenonum aémulos
sections.translation: Aeschrodora , you who have for your patrons the butchers , those rivals of the procurers , who , just like ourselves , by false oaths seek their gains , do you listen ; unless the three larders shall be crammed for me this day with carcases of ample weight , to-morrow , just as they say that formerly the two sons of Jupiter fastened Dirce [*]
